# 4.3 Linear Independence — Extension / Subset Theorems (Recording Transcript)

**Instructor:** Ahmed Ayache
**Source:** Auto-generated transcript (mixed Arabic + English, heavy transcription errors — raw audio is authoritative)
**Supplements:** `notes/4.3 Linear independence.pdf`, `recordings/4.3 Linear Independence.md`

---

## What this lecture covers

After the definition recap, the prof proves **two paired theorems** relating linear (in)dependence to adding/removing vectors from a set. These are *contrapositives* of each other, so logically they are one theorem stated two ways.

> **Theorem A (Extension of dependent = dependent).**
> If $S = \{v_1, \ldots, v_r\}$ is linearly **dependent**, then *every* larger set $S' = S \cup \{u_1, \ldots, u_s\}$ is also linearly **dependent**.

> **Theorem B (Subset of independent = independent).**
> If $S = \{v_1, \ldots, v_r\}$ is linearly **independent**, then *every* subset $T \subseteq S$ is also linearly **independent**.

**These two statements are logically equivalent** (contrapositives) — the prof explicitly invokes this: "$P \Rightarrow Q$ is the same as $\neg Q \Rightarrow \neg P$." You only need to prove one; the other is free.

---

## Proof of Theorem A (extension of dependent stays dependent)

**Given:** $\{v_1, \ldots, v_r\}$ linearly dependent.
**To show:** $\{v_1, \ldots, v_r, u_1, \ldots, u_s\}$ linearly dependent.

Since $\{v_1, \ldots, v_r\}$ is dependent, there exist scalars $a_1, \ldots, a_r$, **not all zero**, with
$$a_1 v_1 + a_2 v_2 + \cdots + a_r v_r = 0_V.$$

Now extend to the bigger set by adding zero coefficients on the new vectors:
$$
a_1 v_1 + a_2 v_2 + \cdots + a_r v_r + 0\cdot u_1 + 0\cdot u_2 + \cdots + 0\cdot u_s = 0_V.
$$

This is a representation of $0_V$ as a linear combination of the extended set. Since at least one $a_i \ne 0$, **not all the coefficients are zero** — so this is a **non-trivial** representation. Hence the extended set is linearly dependent. $\blacksquare$

---

## Proof of Theorem B (subset of independent stays independent)

**Given:** $\{v_1, \ldots, v_r\}$ linearly independent.
**To show:** any subset, say $\{v_1, \ldots, v_h\}$ with $h < r$, is linearly independent.

Suppose $c_1 v_1 + c_2 v_2 + \cdots + c_h v_h = 0_V$.

Pad with zero coefficients on the missing vectors:
$$
c_1 v_1 + \cdots + c_h v_h + 0\cdot v_{h+1} + \cdots + 0\cdot v_r = 0_V.
$$

This is a representation of $0_V$ using all of $\{v_1, \ldots, v_r\}$. Because that larger set is linearly independent **by hypothesis**, the only way this can hold is if *all* coefficients are zero — including the ones on our subset. So $c_1 = c_2 = \cdots = c_h = 0$. Hence the subset is linearly independent. $\blacksquare$

---

## Mnemonic

- **Bigger dependent ← make dependent even bigger:** dependence is *preserved by extension*. ("If you're already stuck, adding more stuff doesn't unstick you.")
- **Smaller independent ← take subset of independent:** independence is *preserved by restriction*. ("If every vector is truly needed, a subset of them are still truly needed.")

**What does NOT hold (common trap):**
- Adding vectors to an **independent** set does NOT preserve independence. (You can always add a dependent copy.)
- Removing vectors from a **dependent** set does NOT preserve dependence. (Removing the redundant vector may make the remainder independent.)

So the **only** valid directions are: extension-of-dependent and subset-of-independent.

## Active recall

- 🟢 State the two theorems in your own words.
- 🟢 Which way does the implication go — subset of **independent** or subset of **dependent**? Extension of **independent** or extension of **dependent**?
- 🟡 Prove: if $\{v_1, \ldots, v_r\}$ is linearly dependent, then so is $\{v_1, \ldots, v_r, u\}$ for any $u$.
- 🟡 Explain why Theorem A and Theorem B are contrapositives of each other.
- 🔴 Counter-examples: give a set $S$ that is dependent but has an independent proper subset. Give a set $T$ that is independent but has a dependent extension.
- 🔴 Does Theorem B imply that any non-empty subset of $\mathbb{R}^n$'s standard basis $\{e_1, \ldots, e_n\}$ is linearly independent? Justify.
